summary: Uncle Bob defends the continued relevance of the SOLID principles against criticism that they are outdated in the age of microservices and reduced inheritance usage. He systematically addresses each principle, arguing that software fundamentals haven't changed since 1945 - it's still sequence, selection, and iteration. He responds specifically to Dan North's 'just write simple code' advice, contending that while the sentiment is correct, simplicity requires disciplined principles to achieve. The core argument is that each generation mistakenly believes their technical context is fundamentally different from their predecessors.
key_points: Software hasn't fundamentally changed since 1945 - it remains if statements, while loops, and assignments, so principles from decades ago still apply
Microservices don't eliminate the need for SRP; you can create tangled microservices just as easily as tangled monoliths
LSP is about sub-typing and interfaces, not just inheritance - all implementations of interfaces are subtypes that users must understand consistently
The advice to 'just write simple code' without guiding principles is how you get complicated messes
Every new generation thinks their world is vastly different; every generation is wrong about that
money_quotes: Software is still if statements, while loops, and assignment statements — Sequence, Selection, and Iteration.
Every new generation likes to think that their world is vastly different from the generation before. Every new generation is wrong about that.
You can create a tangled microservice, or a tangled set of microservices if you mix code that changes for different reasons.
Of all the principles, the idea that anyone would question this one fills me full of dread for the future of our industry.
Simple code is both open and closed.
This principle is about keeping abstractions crisp and well-defined. It is impossible to believe that this is an outmoded concept.
If the years have taught us anything it is that simplicity requires disciplines guided by principles.
The best way to make a complicated mess is to tell everyone to 'just be simple' and give them no further guidance.
key_insight: Simplicity in software is not achieved by vague exhortations but by disciplined adherence to principles that define what simplicity means in practice.
